title: Woven window warp hanging
subtitle: A frame-loom hanging with spaced warp threads that allow light through, for a window display
excerpt: Weave a wall or window hanging on a frame loom using widely-spaced warp threads so gaps between the warp are part of the design. The result is a translucent textile that works as a window display or a light room divider.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional frame-loom and open-warp textile practice. Open-warp hangings are documented in Scandinavian and British textile art traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: The distance between warp threads on the loom. A standard warp has threads close together; an open or spaced warp leaves visible gaps between threads that become part of the textile's structure.
The horizontal thread woven through the warp. In an open-warp hanging, the weft sections are interspersed with unwoven warp gaps to create the translucent effect.
The reduction in warp length caused by the weft interweaving and bending the warp threads. For an open-warp hanging at a loose tension, take-up is minimal.

content.text: sections alternate with unwoven areas in the warp, and the space-to-weft ratio is entirely in your control. Minimal
means the finished piece stays close to the warped dimensions.

items.name: Cotton warp yarn (4 or 6 ply cotton, or linen)
Weft yarn in various textures and weights
A branch or dowel for the top hanging bar

content.text: Wind the warp onto the frame loom leaving 1 to 2 cm between each warp thread, rather than the closer spacing used for standard weaving. The gaps between warp threads will remain open in the finished piece. For a window hanging with pronounced translucency, space threads 2 cm apart; for more opacity, space them 1 cm apart.

Decide where weft sections will appear and where the warp will remain bare. A simple approach is alternating 5 cm woven bands with 3 cm open gaps. Mark the planned positions on the warp with a pencil line across the warp frame before beginning to weave.

Weave each planned band in plain weave, using a varied selection of yarns for texture interest: a smooth cotton for some bands, a textured mohair or bouclé for others. Beat lightly: open-warp hangings look better with a relaxed beat that allows the weft to sit naturally in the wide sett.

Cut the hanging from the loom, leaving at least 8 cm of warp at the top and bottom for fringe. At the top, knot or braid pairs of warp threads together and loop them over a branch or dowel. At the bottom, trim fringe to a consistent length or knot in groups.

items.problem: The woven bands look dense and opaque, losing the open feel
items.cause: The weft was beaten too firmly, pushing the weft threads close together and filling the sett
items.fix: Beat very lightly and allow the weft to lie in the shed at a natural angle without compressing it. The open feel comes from restraint in the beat.

items.problem: The warp threads are pulling together in the woven sections, narrowing the piece
items.cause: The weft was not given enough arc when inserted, pulling the selvedge warp threads inward
items.fix: Insert the weft in a gentle diagonal arc across the width before beating. The extra length of weft accommodates the selvedge threads without drawing them in.
